title: The Science of Self-Compassion
author: Dr Sarah Mitchell
excerpt: Research consistently shows that self-compassion — treating yourself with the same kindness you'd offer a good friend — is one of the most powerful tools for psychological wellbeing.
content:
What Is Self-Compassion?

Research consistently shows that self-compassion — treating yourself with the same kindness you'd offer a good friend — is one of the most powerful tools for psychological wellbeing. Yet many of us find it far easier to extend warmth to others than to ourselves.

Dr Kristin Neff, a pioneering researcher in this field, defines self-compassion as having three core components: self-kindness (being warm and understanding toward ourselves when we suffer), common humanity (recognising that suffering and personal inadequacy is part of the shared human experience), and mindfulness (holding our painful thoughts and feelings in balanced awareness).

Why Self-Criticism Backfires

Many people believe that being hard on themselves is what keeps them motivated and high-performing. The research tells a different story. Chronic self-criticism activates the body's threat-defence system — the same fight-or-flight response triggered by external danger. This flood of cortisol and adrenaline is useful in short bursts but damaging when sustained.

In contrast, self-compassion activates the care system — associated with feelings of safety, warmth, and connection. This state is actually more conducive to learning from mistakes, taking healthy risks, and maintaining motivation over the long term.

Practising Self-Compassion

Self-compassion is a skill that can be developed. Some evidence-based starting points include noticing when you're being self-critical (mindful awareness), asking "what would I say to a friend in this situation?", placing a hand on your heart and acknowledging that this moment is difficult, and using self-compassionate journalling to process difficult experiences.

When to Seek Support

If you find self-criticism is significantly impacting your wellbeing, mood, or relationships, speaking with a psychologist can help. Therapies such as Compassion-Focused Therapy (CFT) and Acceptance and Commitment Therapy (ACT) include structured approaches to building self-compassion as a core therapeutic goal.

title: AI and Mental Health: What You Need to Know
author: Mood & Mind Centre Team
excerpt: Artificial intelligence is increasingly present in mental health spaces — from chatbot apps to AI-assisted diagnosis tools. We explore what the evidence says.
content:
The Rise of AI in Mental Health

Artificial intelligence is increasingly present in mental health spaces — from chatbot apps to AI-assisted diagnosis tools. As psychologists, we're watching these developments closely, with both curiosity and caution.

What AI Can and Can't Do

Current AI tools can provide psychoeducation, help track mood patterns, offer scripted coping strategies, and provide immediate availability — particularly valuable for people in crisis who need support at 2am. For mild to moderate presentations, some digital tools show promising outcomes in research settings.

However, AI cannot build a genuine therapeutic relationship. It cannot pick up on the subtle non-verbal cues that an experienced clinician notices. It cannot adapt flexibly to complex, co-occurring presentations. And it cannot make clinical judgements that account for the full context of a person's life and history.

Our Clinical Perspective

We see AI tools as potentially useful adjuncts — not replacements — for psychological care. Used well, they might extend the reach of support between sessions, help with homework tasks, or provide access to psychoeducation. Used poorly, they risk giving people false confidence that their mental health needs are being addressed when they require more substantial care.

Questions to Ask About Any Mental Health App

What is the evidence base? Has this tool been tested in clinical trials? Who developed it, and were mental health professionals involved? What happens with your data? Does it recognise when to refer to a human professional? If you're unsure whether a digital tool is right for you, talk with your psychologist.

title: Perimenopause and Mental Health
author: Dr Emily Chen
excerpt: The hormonal changes of perimenopause can have a profound impact on mood, cognition, and psychological wellbeing. Yet the mental health dimension of this life stage is still frequently overlooked.
content:
Understanding Perimenopause

The hormonal changes of perimenopause can have a profound impact on mood, cognition, and psychological wellbeing. Yet the mental health dimension of this life stage is still frequently overlooked — both by medical professionals and by women themselves, who may not connect their symptoms to hormonal shifts.

What the Research Shows

Oestrogen plays a significant role in regulating serotonin, dopamine, and other neurotransmitters central to mood. As oestrogen levels fluctuate during perimenopause, many women experience anxiety, low mood, irritability, cognitive changes (often called "brain fog"), sleep disruption, and reduced stress tolerance — even if they've never had mental health difficulties before.

Research shows that women are two to four times more likely to experience a depressive episode during perimenopause than at other times of their adult life. Importantly, this is not simply a response to life stressors — there are neurobiological drivers at play.

Psychological Support During This Transition

Effective psychological support during perimenopause typically involves psychoeducation (understanding what's happening and why), evidence-based approaches for mood and anxiety (CBT, ACT, mindfulness-based interventions), sleep strategies, and values-based work to navigate identity shifts that often accompany this life stage. We work collaboratively with GPs and gynaecologists where appropriate.

You Don't Have to Navigate This Alone

If you're noticing significant changes in your mood, anxiety levels, sleep, or cognition and are in the perimenopause transition, please reach out. These symptoms are real, they have neurobiological explanations, and evidence-based support is available.

title: Everyday Anxiety: When Worry Becomes a Problem
author: Mood & Mind Centre Team
excerpt: Anxiety is a normal human experience — but when it starts interfering with daily life, relationships, or work, it may be time to seek support.
content:
The Purpose of Anxiety

Anxiety is not the enemy. It's an ancient survival mechanism designed to keep us safe — to prompt us to prepare, to notice danger, to act when action is needed. The problem arises when the alarm system becomes oversensitive, triggering in situations that don't warrant it, or staying activated long after the threat has passed.

When Does Worry Become a Problem?

Some key signs that anxiety has moved beyond helpful into problematic include: difficulty controlling worry even when you want to stop, physical symptoms like tension, headaches, or gut issues, avoidance of situations or activities because of fear, sleep disruption due to racing thoughts, and anxiety that's significantly impacting work, relationships, or quality of life.

Evidence-Based Approaches

The good news is that anxiety is one of the most treatable psychological conditions. Cognitive Behaviour Therapy (CBT) has decades of evidence behind it, helping people identify and restructure unhelpful thought patterns and gradually face avoided situations. Acceptance and Commitment Therapy (ACT) and mindfulness-based approaches are also well-supported.

Getting Support

If anxiety is getting in the way of living the life you want, psychological support can make a significant difference. Our team at Mood & Mind Centre has extensive experience working with anxiety in all its forms — from generalised worry to panic, social anxiety, health anxiety, and phobias.

title: Recognising Depression: Beyond Feeling Sad
author: Mood & Mind Centre Team
excerpt: Depression is much more than persistent sadness. Understanding its many faces can be the first step toward getting the right support.
content:
Depression Is More Than Sadness

One of the most common misconceptions about depression is that it always looks like profound sadness or tearfulness. In reality, depression presents in many different ways — and some of its most common expressions are easy to miss or misattribute to other causes.

The Many Faces of Depression

Depression can manifest as persistent low energy or fatigue, loss of interest or pleasure in activities that used to be enjoyable (anhedonia), changes in appetite or sleep, difficulty concentrating or making decisions, irritability or short temper (particularly common in men), physical aches and pains with no clear cause, withdrawing from people and activities, and feelings of worthlessness, hopelessness, or excessive guilt.

High-Functioning Depression

Some people continue to meet their responsibilities at work and at home while experiencing significant depressive symptoms — a pattern sometimes called high-functioning depression. Because these individuals appear to be coping, their distress is often invisible to others and sometimes even to themselves. The gap between how they appear on the outside and feel on the inside can itself become a source of shame.

When to Seek Help

If you've been experiencing several of the above symptoms for more than two weeks, or if you're having thoughts of harming yourself, please reach out for support. Depression is not a character flaw or weakness — it's a medical condition that responds well to evidence-based psychological treatment.

title: Burnout: More Than Just Tiredness
author: Mood & Mind Centre Team
excerpt: Burnout has become one of the defining mental health challenges of our time. Understanding what it really is — and isn't — is essential to recovery.
content:
What Is Burnout?

Burnout is a state of chronic exhaustion resulting from prolonged exposure to demanding, high-stress situations — most often (but not exclusively) in the context of work. It was formally recognised by the World Health Organisation in 2019 as an occupational phenomenon, characterised by three dimensions: exhaustion, cynicism or detachment, and reduced sense of efficacy.

Burnout vs Depression

Burnout and depression share features and frequently co-occur, but they're distinct. Burnout tends to be context-specific — people often feel better when removed from the stressor. Depression typically pervades all areas of life. That said, prolonged burnout can trigger a depressive episode, which is why early intervention matters.

The Road to Recovery

Recovery from burnout is rarely as simple as taking a holiday. Sustainable recovery typically requires addressing the structural contributors (workload, autonomy, support, fairness, values alignment), rebuilding protective factors like sleep, social connection, and meaning, and often psychological support to process the impact and rebuild resilience. Values clarification work — understanding what matters most and realigning life accordingly — is frequently a core component.

Preventing Burnout

Prevention requires attention to early warning signs: persistent tiredness that sleep doesn't fix, growing cynicism, feeling disconnected from your work or purpose, reduced performance despite effort, and increased physical symptoms. Recognising these early and making adjustments — before reaching full burnout — is always easier than recovery after the fact.

title: Sleep and Mental Health: The Two-Way Street
author: Mood & Mind Centre Team
excerpt: Poor sleep and poor mental health have a bidirectional relationship — each makes the other worse. Understanding this cycle is key to breaking it.
content:
The Sleep-Mental Health Relationship

The relationship between sleep and mental health runs in both directions. Anxiety can make it hard to fall asleep; insomnia can worsen anxiety. Depression often disrupts sleep; poor sleep can trigger or deepen depression. Understanding this bidirectional relationship is essential to addressing either effectively.

What Happens When We Sleep

Sleep is far from passive. During sleep, the brain consolidates memories, processes emotional experiences, clears metabolic waste products, and regulates mood and stress hormones. Consistently poor sleep disrupts all of these functions — impairing emotional regulation, reducing distress tolerance, and increasing reactivity to stressors.

Cognitive Behavioural Therapy for Insomnia

CBT-I (Cognitive Behavioural Therapy for Insomnia) is now recognised as the gold-standard first-line treatment for chronic insomnia — more effective in the long term than sleep medication. It addresses the thoughts and behaviours that perpetuate poor sleep, including sleep-related anxiety, irregular sleep schedules, and unhelpful coping strategies like excessive time in bed.

Sleep Hygiene: Necessary But Not Sufficient

You've likely heard the advice: limit screens before bed, keep a consistent schedule, avoid caffeine in the afternoon. This "sleep hygiene" advice isn't wrong — but it's rarely sufficient for entrenched insomnia. If you've tried the basics and your sleep remains significantly disrupted, working with a psychologist trained in CBT-I can make a substantial difference.

title: Talking to Kids About Mental Health
author: Mood & Mind Centre Team
excerpt: Starting conversations about feelings and mental health with children can feel daunting. Here's how to approach these important discussions in age-appropriate ways.
content:
Why These Conversations Matter

Children who grow up in families where emotions are named, validated, and discussed openly develop better emotional regulation, stronger relationships, and greater resilience. Conversely, when feelings are consistently minimised or avoided, children learn that emotions are dangerous or shameful — a belief that can shape their mental health for decades.

Age-Appropriate Conversations

With young children (3-6), keep it simple and concrete: name feelings, validate them, and model your own ("I'm feeling a bit worried about that — I'm going to take some deep breaths"). With primary school-age children, you can introduce more nuance — the idea that we can have big feelings and still choose how we respond. Adolescents benefit from conversations that respect their growing autonomy and don't rush to fix or minimise.

When a Child Is Struggling

Signs that a child may need more support include significant changes in behaviour or mood, withdrawal from friends or activities they used to enjoy, declining school performance, physical complaints (headaches, stomach aches) with no medical cause, sleep changes, and expressions of hopelessness or self-criticism. Trust your instincts as a parent — if something feels different, it's worth exploring.

Getting Professional Support

Seeking help for a child is a sign of strength, not failure. Child and adolescent psychologists are trained to work with young people in developmentally appropriate ways. Early support for childhood mental health difficulties tends to produce better outcomes than waiting until problems are more entrenched.

title: Navigating Conflict in Relationships
author: Mood & Mind Centre Team
excerpt: Conflict is inevitable in any close relationship. The question isn't whether you'll disagree — it's whether you can do it in ways that preserve connection.
content:
Conflict Is Normal

Research by Dr John Gottman — who has studied couples for decades — found that the presence of conflict doesn't predict relationship failure. What matters is how conflict is handled. In fact, the ability to repair after conflict is one of the strongest predictors of long-term relationship health.

The Four Horsemen

Gottman identified four communication patterns that are particularly damaging to relationships: criticism (attacking the person's character rather than raising a specific complaint), contempt (expressions of superiority or disrespect), defensiveness (self-protection rather than taking responsibility), and stonewalling (emotional withdrawal). Recognising these patterns in yourself and your partner is the first step to changing them.

Skills for Healthier Conflict

Effective conflict in relationships involves raising issues softly rather than with blame or criticism, staying with one issue at a time rather than bringing in past grievances, taking breaks when physiological arousal (racing heart, shallow breathing) makes productive conversation impossible, listening to understand rather than to rebut, and repairing early — a "time out" works only if it's followed by a genuine return to the conversation.

When to Seek Couples Support

Couples therapy is most effective when sought early — before patterns are deeply entrenched and before significant emotional withdrawal has occurred. If you're noticing recurring conflict that doesn't resolve, growing distance, or a sense that you're no longer a team, relationship support can help rebuild connection and communication.

title: Trauma and the Body
author: Mood & Mind Centre Team
excerpt: Trauma isn't just a psychological experience — it lives in the body. Understanding the somatic dimension of trauma is transforming how we approach treatment.
content:
The Body Keeps the Score

The title of Bessel van der Kolk's landmark book — "The Body Keeps the Score" — captures something clinicians have long observed: trauma isn't just stored in memory and meaning-making. It lives in the nervous system, in patterns of muscle tension, in the way the body braces or collapses under stress.

What Happens in the Body During Trauma

When we experience something overwhelming, the brain's threat-detection system activates a cascade of physiological responses — accelerated heart rate, shallow breathing, muscle tension, altered digestion. In a healthy stress response, these changes resolve when the threat passes. With trauma, the nervous system can remain stuck in states of hyperarousal (always on alert) or hypoarousal (shutdown, numbness) long after the event.

Body-Informed Treatment Approaches

Increasingly, trauma treatment incorporates attention to the body alongside cognitive and narrative approaches. EMDR (Eye Movement Desensitisation and Reprocessing) works with the body's information-processing system. Somatic therapies directly address bodily held trauma. Even trauma-focused CBT incorporates attention to physical sensations as part of processing difficult memories.

What This Means for Recovery

Understanding the somatic dimension of trauma helps explain why talking alone is sometimes insufficient. Recovery often involves helping the nervous system find safety — through breath work, grounding techniques, gradual exposure, and titrated processing of traumatic memories. With appropriate support, the nervous system can learn that the past is over, and genuine recovery is possible.
